title: Blind Spot Check Guide
summary: Understand coverage gaps, obstructions, camera overlap, scene geometry, and missed activity risk.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps identify whether a camera layout may leave uncovered or weakly covered areas.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch camera design problems before installation.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when reviewing camera layouts, adding cameras, checking perimeter coverage, or validating whether a scene is covered from enough angles.
bullets: Early camera layout planning.
Expansion or retrofit review.
Existing-site validation.
Explaining coverage and detail tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical physical security and camera planning terms.

items.label: Coverage Area
items.description: The physical area the cameras are expected to monitor.
items.whyItMatters: Coverage area defines what must be visible.
items.commonMistake: Checking camera count without defining the target area.

items.label: Camera Views
items.description: The number and direction of camera views covering the area.
items.whyItMatters: Multiple views can reduce blind spot risk.
items.commonMistake: Assuming one camera angle sees everything.

items.label: Obstructions
items.description: Objects that block or reduce visibility.
items.whyItMatters: Obstructions can create blind spots even inside the field of view.
items.commonMistake: Designing from a clean drawing without real-world obstacles.

items.label: Overlap
items.whyItMatters: Overlap helps maintain visibility if one view is blocked.

items.label: Target Priority
items.description: Areas or activities that matter most.
items.whyItMatters: Priority determines which blind spots matter most.

items.label: Mounting Angle
items.description: The camera angle relative to the scene.
items.whyItMatters: Angle affects whether objects or people hide behind each other.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring perspective and only looking at field-of-view lines.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means blind spot risk appears low. Watch means obstructions or overlap should be reviewed. Risk means the design may miss activity in important areas.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears aligned with the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but placement, lens choice, or detail should be reviewed.
Risk: adjust camera position, lens choice, lighting, spacing, or coverage expectations before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most camera planning problems come from confusing visible coverage with useful detail. A camera may see an area without capturing enough detail for the intended task.
bullets: Using one wide camera where a dedicated detail view is needed.
Ignoring target distance, angle, lighting, and obstructions.
Expecting overview cameras to perform identification tasks.
Failing to document the design goal for each camera.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, site testing, lighting review, privacy requirements, or project-specific design judgment.
bullets: Camera specs still need to be validated against the actual model.
Lighting and mounting conditions can change real image quality.
A good design usually separates overview views from detail views.
Field testing remains important for critical camera views.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, site-specific testing, code/privacy review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual camera, lens, lighting, and mounting location.
Confirm owner expectations and security objectives.
Document assumptions before implementation.
